rulings.Case Title: JONATHAN HARMS VS. A16 ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs
rulings.Rulings:
Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2024, Line 2. 

PETITIONER JONATHAN HARMS' Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs.

Granted as unopposed.

For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely.  Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.

Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.  The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.  The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing.  Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.

The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department.  Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely.  A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts.  If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address.
=(302/CK)

rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE ET AL VS. SUTTER HEALTH, A CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Deposition Notices And Joinders And To Amend Protective Order To Sequence Second Day Of Plaintiffs' Depositions Or, In The Alternative, For A
Sequencing Order
rulings.Rulings:
Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2024, Line 3. 

3 - PLAINTIFF JANE DOE AND JOHN DOE's Motion To Quash Deposition Notices And Joinders And To Amend Protective Order To Sequence Second Day Of Plaintiffs' Depositions Or, In The Alternative, For A Sequencing Order.

Denied. 
	There is no good cause to quash the notices of deposition.   There is no practical need for "clarity" regarding these notices. The issue of whether the subsequent days of depositions should go forward has already been decided, and there is no good cause presented to avoid the effect of that order; specifically, no cause to "sequence" the remaining deposition time to await the resolution of the Kachru appeal.  The court notes that the lead case was filed October 2021, with the five year statute likely expiring in 2026.  In the meantime, all discovery must be complete, the experts prepared, parties allowed to make their motions for summary judgment or adjudication, First State Ins. Co. v. Superior Ct., 79 Cal. App. 4th 324, 336 (2000) (right to file such motion), all before trial preparation in a complex multi-party case. One would think that delay would be plaintiff's enemy here. 
	Disability accommodation. This is denied.  CRC 1.100 contemplates accommodation in the provision of court services, such as the ability to attend hearings. It is expressly designed to allow full and equal access to the judicial system.  Delaying depositions do not come within its scope; there are adequate procedures in the Discovery Code to allow for a wide variety of equitable and related considerations to impact deposition scheduling and the conditions under which they will be taken. 
For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely.  Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.

Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.  The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.  The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing.  Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.

The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department.  Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely.  A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts.  If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address.
=(302/CK)

rulings.Case Title: DOUGLAS N. AKAY VS. SIAMAK TAROMI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion For Relief From Default
rulings.Rulings:
Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2024, Line 4. 

DEFENDANT SIAMAK TAROMI's Motion For Relief From Default.

Granted.

Defendant's evidence shows he was out of the county and surprised when he found out that he had been served by publication.  He retained a lawyer (Cassutt) to investigate the court files, who then proceeded promptly to prepare an Answer.  Defendant was technically in default as of about May 13, before Cassutt found out the status of the case.  On May 22, Cassutt told plaintiff's counsel he was ready to file an answer. On the same day the clerk rejected a request for entry of default. At that point if not sooner, plaintiff should have readily agreed to allow the filing and service of an Answer, even though defendant was technically in default.  Counsel have an obligation to inform the other side if they intend to take a default, Shapell Socal Rental Properties, LLC v. Chico's FAS, Inc., 85 Cal. App. 5th 198, 214 (2022).  Here,  plaintiff was actually in touch with defense counsel, and should have worked out an arrangement to allow the Answer to be filed. (I do not suggest that defense counsel's behavior here was comparable to that in Shapell.)  Instead, plaintiff again sought to have default entered. The only reason, it seems, that the plaintiff declined to allow the Answer and pursued repeated efforts to have the default entered was the high costs and time incurred in trying to get the defendant served. That, however, is not a good reason to decline to agree to allow the defendant to appear. Those costs are likely recoverable. E.g., CCP 415.30(d). They should not be a factor on whether the case is resolved on the merits.

For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely.  Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.

Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.  The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.  The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing.  Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.

The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department.  Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely.  A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts.  If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address.
=(302/CK)
